title: Tool Documentation Quality
concept: Why writing clear docstrings for tools is one of the highest leverage ways to improve agent accuracy.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: how does a router decide which tool matches what the agent wants to do?

options.text: It picks a tool at random every time
It matches the agent's intent to a tool using classification or embeddings
It always uses the first tool in the list

exercises.explain: A router reads the agent's intent and finds the closest-matching tool, often using classification or embedding similarity. That's how the right tool gets picked for the job.

cards.title: How does the agent know?
cards.body: An agent can't see inside a tool. It only reads the tool's description to decide when to use it. Bad description, wrong choice.

cards.title: What is a docstring?
cards.body: A docstring is the written description of what a tool does, what inputs it needs, and what it returns. It's the tool's instruction label for the agent.

cards.title: Labels on a toolbox
cards.body: Imagine a toolbox where every tool has a clear label: 'cuts wood', 'tightens screws'. A helper grabs the right one fast. Blank or vague labels make them guess.

cards.title: Vague vs clear
cards.body: Vague: 'gets data'. Clear: 'get_weather: returns today's temperature and rain chance for a given city.' The clear one tells the agent exactly when to reach for it.

cards.title: Myth: docs are just for humans
cards.body: Not true for agents! The agent literally reads the docstring to decide. Improving your descriptions is one of the cheapest, highest-leverage accuracy boosts.

cards.title: Which tool gets picked?
cards.body: Same tool, different labels. Clear docs guide the agent; vague docs cause wrong picks and errors.

left.text: 'does stuff' - agent guesses and often picks the wrong tool

right.text: 'converts USD to EUR' - agent picks confidently and correctly

exercises.prompt: Why is writing a clear docstring one of the highest-leverage ways to improve an agent?

options.text: It makes the tool run faster
The agent relies on the description to choose the right tool, so clarity means better choices
It changes the tool's output color
It hides the tool from the agent

exercises.explain: The agent decides which tool to use based on the docstring. A clear description directly leads to more correct tool choices with very little effort to write.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A vague tool description can cause the agent to pick the wrong tool.
exercises.explain: True. If the description doesn't clearly say what the tool does, the agent may misjudge when to use it and pick the wrong one.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about tool documentation.
exercises.sentence: The agent reads a tool's docstring to decide when to use it.
exercises.explain: The docstring is the written description the agent reads to decide when a tool fits the task.

exercises.prompt: Which tool description was written by an AI to be clear and complete?

options.text: get_stock_price: returns the latest trading price for a given company ticker symbol.

exercises.explain: The AI-written option names the tool, states exactly what it returns, and names the input it needs. Clear, complete docstrings help the agent choose correctly.

exercises.prompt: An agent keeps calling the wrong tool. Two tools exist: 'search' (docstring: 'find things') and 'send_email' (docstring: 'find things'). What's the best fix, and why?

options.text: Add more tools so it has more choices
Rewrite each docstring to clearly describe its unique job, so the agent can tell them apart
Remove all docstrings so the agent guesses

exercises.explain: The agent picks tools by reading their descriptions. When two docstrings are identical and vague, it can't tell them apart. Writing clear, distinct descriptions ('search: look up web results for a query' vs 'send_email: send a message to a recipient') is a tiny change that massively improves accuracy. That's why docstring quality is such high-leverage work.
